name: Coalition Mobilization
consequences: Pulling this lever expands stakeholder network, increasing pressure on procurement decisions. It also raises coordination overhead and may dilute technical focus. It can create a platform for sustained advocacy but may slow decision cycles.
options: Engage a cross‑sector coalition of civil‑society, academia, and industry to co‑author a white paper.
Launch a public challenge inviting NGOs to propose alternative authentication models.
Partner with a local university to host a hackathon focused on platform‑neutral identity.
review: Core tension: balancing broad stakeholder buy‑in against focused technical expertise. Weakness: The options overlook the need for sustained engagement beyond initial coalition formation.

name: Demonstrator Proof‑of‑Concept
consequences: Pulling this lever provides tangible evidence of fallback viability, strengthening policy arguments. It requires dedicated engineering resources and may delay other phases if scope expands. Successful demos can accelerate procurement language adoption but risk over‑engineering.
options: Develop a FIDO2/WebAuthn demonstrator on GrapheneOS.
Create a browser‑based fallback using WebAuthn and a hardware token.
Prototype a lightweight server‑side identity broker that abstracts platform signals.
review: Core tension: proving technical feasibility while keeping the project lean. Weakness: The options do not address the need for secure, production‑grade certification.

name: Procurement Language Design
consequences: Pulling this lever embeds platform‑neutral requirements into future contracts, reducing long‑term supplier concentration. It may face resistance from incumbent vendors and require iterative negotiation. It can also set a precedent for other national identity programs.
options: Draft procurement specifications that mandate platform‑neutral fallback.
Propose a modular architecture clause that allows future platform changes.
Introduce a penalty clause for exclusive platform dependency.
review: Core tension: enforcing neutrality while maintaining procurement flexibility. Weakness: The options ignore potential legal challenges from existing platform providers.

name: Regulatory Alignment
consequences: Pulling this lever aligns the project with national and EU regulatory frameworks, easing approval pathways. It may require additional legal analysis and could slow momentum if regulatory cycles are long. It also opens avenues for funding and cross‑border collaboration.
options: Submit a formal regulatory brief to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen.
Request a regulatory sandbox for platform‑neutral identity.
Advocate for a new EU directive on digital identity resilience.
review: Core tension: navigating complex regulatory environments while pursuing rapid impact. Weakness: The options fail to consider the time lag inherent in policy adoption.

name: Budget Contingency
consequences: Pulling this lever safeguards the project against funding gaps and scope creep. It may reduce available capital for technical work and could lead to stricter milestone enforcement. It also signals fiscal prudence to stakeholders.
options: Allocate a contingency reserve of 1.5 million DKK for unforeseen regulatory delays.
Implement a phased funding release tied to milestone approvals.
Secure a co‑funding agreement with a European research grant.
review: Core tension: balancing financial risk mitigation with resource availability. Weakness: The options do not address potential delays in grant disbursement.

name: EU Standards Advocacy
consequences: Pulling this lever positions Denmark as a leader in platform‑neutral identity, potentially influencing EU policy. It requires sustained engagement with EU bodies and may divert focus from national procurement. It can also attract additional funding and expertise.
options: Contribute to the European Digital Identity Wallet working group.
Publish a technical paper on fallback authentication for EU adoption.
Lobby for EU funding for platform‑neutral identity research.
review: Core tension: aligning national objectives with EU standards while maintaining domestic momentum. Weakness: The options overlook the need for a coordinated EU‑wide lobbying strategy.

name: Platform Integrity Redundancy
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the set of trusted platform signals beyond Apple and Google, requiring new verification channels. This increases the complexity of the trust model and may slow integration timelines, but it reduces single‑point failure risk. It also necessitates new certification processes for third‑party integrity providers.
options: Establish a cross‑vendor verification consortium that aggregates device integrity signals from open‑source OS projects and hardware manufacturers.
Deploy a lightweight, on‑device attestation service that operates independently of mobile OS APIs and reports directly to the national identity authority.
Introduce a blockchain‑based audit trail that records platform integrity proofs, enabling transparent verification without relying on proprietary vendor infrastructure.
review: Core tension: balancing expanded platform trust signals against increased system complexity. Weakness: options overlook the need for vendor‑agnostic certification standards.

name: Fallback Authentication Architecture
consequences: Pulling this lever creates an alternative authentication pathway that can be invoked when primary mobile OS channels fail. This adds redundancy but may introduce latency and require additional user training. It also necessitates coordination with existing identity providers to ensure seamless handover.
options: Design a browser‑based authentication flow that leverages WebAuthn and OpenID Connect, allowing users to authenticate through any modern web browser without mobile app dependencies.
Implement a hardware‑token fallback system that issues time‑bound QR codes, enabling users to scan with a secure device and complete authentication even when mobile OS services are unavailable.
Create a server‑side fallback service that temporarily accepts biometric data via a dedicated kiosk, providing an emergency authentication channel during platform outages.
review: Core tension: ensuring fallback reliability while maintaining user convenience. Weakness: options ignore the operational cost of deploying and maintaining fallback infrastructure.

name: Stakeholder Engagement Amplification
consequences: Pulling this lever expands the coalition of civil‑society, academia, and industry partners, increasing pressure on procurement bodies. This may accelerate policy adoption but could also dilute focus and create coordination overhead. It also risks politicizing the technical agenda.
options: Launch a multi‑disciplinary think‑tank that convenes privacy scholars, security engineers, and public‑policy experts to co‑author a platform‑neutral white paper.
Organize a nationwide hackathon that invites developers to prototype fallback authentication solutions, fostering grassroots innovation and generating public‑support evidence.
Establish a public‑consultation portal that collects structured feedback from citizens on platform‑neutral requirements, feeding data into the policy proposal.
review: Core tension: broadening stakeholder input versus maintaining strategic focus. Weakness: options fail to address potential conflicts between divergent stakeholder priorities.

name: Procurement Language Diversification
consequences: Pulling this lever modifies procurement documents to include explicit language that prohibits exclusive reliance on foreign platform APIs. This may slow initial procurement cycles but creates a legal framework that enforces platform neutrality. It also raises the bar for vendor compliance and may increase upfront evaluation costs.
options: Draft a procurement clause that mandates the inclusion of open‑source integrity verification modules, ensuring that any vendor must provide platform‑agnostic attestations.
Introduce a mandatory certification requirement for vendors to demonstrate compliance with a national fallback authentication standard before bidding.
Embed a penalty schedule in the contract that imposes financial penalties for any failure to deliver platform‑neutral authentication within defined milestones.
review: Core tension: enforcing platform neutrality through procurement versus potential vendor resistance. Weakness: options overlook the need for clear enforcement mechanisms beyond contractual language.

name: Regulatory Resilience Blueprint
consequences: Pulling this lever develops a regulatory framework that requires digital identity systems to demonstrate resilience against platform disruptions. This may increase compliance costs but provides a systematic risk assessment process. It also encourages continuous monitoring and reporting of platform‑related risks.
options: Create a resilience assessment toolkit that mandates periodic testing of fallback authentication pathways under simulated platform outage scenarios.
Publish a regulatory guidance document that defines acceptable risk thresholds for platform dependency and prescribes mitigation strategies.
Establish a cross‑agency oversight committee that reviews platform‑neutral compliance reports and recommends corrective actions.
review: Core tension: formalizing resilience requirements versus administrative burden. Weakness: options ignore the challenge of aligning multiple agencies under a unified framework.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Pulling this lever positions Denmark’s platform‑neutral goals within the broader European Digital Identity Wallet ecosystem. This may require additional coordination with EU working groups but can lead to harmonized standards that benefit national procurement. It also raises Denmark’s influence in shaping future EU policy.
options: Contribute a technical annex to the EU Digital Identity Wallet draft that specifies platform‑neutral fallback authentication requirements.
Lead a joint research initiative with EU member states to develop a shared fallback authentication protocol that can be adopted across national identity systems.
Organize a pan‑European workshop that brings together policymakers, technologists, and civil‑society advocates to align on platform‑neutral procurement language.
review: Core tension: aligning national platform neutrality with EU standards versus potential dilution of specific national requirements. Weakness: options do not address the need for a clear EU‑level enforcement mechanism.

name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
consequences: Pulling this lever reduces reliance on Apple/Google by diversifying platform providers. This may increase integration complexity and require additional certification efforts, potentially delaying rollout. It also spreads risk across multiple vendors, improving resilience but raising coordination overhead.
options: Establish a formal partnership with a domestic mobile OS developer to create a certified app distribution channel that bypasses foreign app stores.
Negotiate a multi‑vendor distribution agreement that allows Danish public‑sector apps to be pre‑installed on certified hardware devices from several suppliers.
Implement a secure, open‑source app store hosted on a government‑managed server that serves vetted applications to citizen devices.
review: Controls vendor concentration vs. integration simplicity. Weakness: The options overlook the need for ongoing compliance audits across multiple vendors.

name: Platform‑Neutral Access Advocacy
consequences: Pulling this lever pushes procurement documents to require platform‑neutral authentication methods. This may slow procurement cycles and increase initial development costs but ultimately reduces long‑term dependency on single vendors. It also encourages broader industry participation and may lead to more innovative solutions.
options: Draft a procurement specification that mandates support for FIDO2/WebAuthn and excludes mandatory reliance on platform‑specific integrity APIs.
Advocate for a certification program that rewards vendors offering platform‑neutral fallback authentication pathways in their product suites.
Publish a white paper demonstrating the security and usability benefits of platform‑neutral authentication to influence policy makers and procurement officials.
review: Balances platform dependency vs. procurement flexibility. Weakness: The options do not address potential resistance from incumbent vendors who benefit from current lock‑in.

name: Resilience‑Centric Procurement
consequences: Pulling this lever embeds resilience criteria into procurement, ensuring continuity plans are mandatory. This may increase upfront costs and extend evaluation periods but reduces risk of service disruption. It also signals to suppliers that resilience is a core value, potentially attracting more robust solutions.
options: Introduce a resilience scoring rubric that evaluates suppliers on their ability to provide platform‑neutral fallback authentication during service outages.
Require suppliers to submit a documented continuity plan that includes multi‑channel authentication options beyond mobile OS platforms.
Mandate periodic resilience drills with suppliers to test fallback authentication pathways under simulated platform shutdown scenarios.
review: Encourages resilience over cost savings. Weakness: The options ignore the potential for suppliers to over‑complicate compliance documentation.

name: Digital Sovereignty Enforcement
consequences: Pulling this lever formalizes legal requirements for domestic control over identity data flows. This may create regulatory friction and require extensive stakeholder consultation but ultimately limits foreign influence on data handling. It also strengthens public trust but could slow innovation if not balanced.
options: Draft a national regulation that mandates all digital identity authentication services to process data within Danish jurisdiction and use Danish‑hosted servers.
Establish a certification body that audits identity providers for compliance with Danish data sovereignty standards and publishes transparency reports.
Create a public‑sector data‑sharing framework that restricts cross‑border data flows unless explicit consent is obtained from Danish citizens.
review: Balances sovereignty vs. operational flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on international interoperability agreements.

name: Stakeholder Alignment Strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever aligns diverse actors around a shared resilience narrative. This may require significant outreach and compromise but can build a coalition that pressures procurement decisions. It also reduces political risk but may dilute specific technical goals.
options: Organize a series of cross‑sector workshops that bring together civil‑society groups, academia, and public‑sector officials to co‑create a resilience charter.
Launch a public‑engagement campaign that highlights the risks of platform lock‑in and solicits citizen feedback on fallback authentication preferences.
Develop a stakeholder‑led advisory board that reviews procurement drafts and ensures alignment with resilience and sovereignty objectives.
review: Promotes coalition building vs. technical focus. Weakness: The options overlook the need for sustained engagement beyond initial workshops.

name: Technical Feasibility Validation
consequences: Pulling this lever produces demonstrable proof that fallback authentication works in realistic environments. This may require specialized hardware and testing resources but provides credibility to policy arguments. It also reduces uncertainty for procurement but may delay policy drafting if technical hurdles arise.
options: Build a low‑cost, hardware‑token prototype that authenticates against a simulated public‑sector identity provider using open‑source firmware.
Deploy a browser‑based fallback authentication module that leverages WebAuthn and server‑side challenge‑response to bypass mobile OS integrity checks.
Conduct a penetration‑testing campaign on the fallback authentication flow to identify potential attack vectors and strengthen the design.
review: Focuses on technical proof vs. policy readiness. Weakness: The options do not address the scalability of the demonstrators for national deployment.
